mnemonics.title: Cache Mapping Techniques
mnemonics.content: D-A-S-H: Direct, Associative, Set - Remember DASH for speed - Direct is simple, Associative is flexible, Set is balanced. Direct maps like addresses (index), Associative anywhere (compares tags), Set maps to sets then anywhere within.

mnemonics.title: Cache Performance Formula
mnemonics.content: AMAT: Average Memory Access Time = Hit Time + Miss Rate × Miss Penalty. Think 'Average' = 'Hit' + 'Miss' × 'Penalty' - like paying the price for missing.

mnemonics.title: Locality Types
mnemonics.content: TEAM: Temporal - Everything Accessed More (again). Spatial - Same data near each other. Remember: Temporal relates to TIME, Spatial relates to SPACE (nearby addresses).

acronyms.usage: Cache replacement policy that evicts the least recently accessed block when cache is full
Cache replacement policy that evicts the oldest block regardless of access pattern
Evicts block that hasn't been accessed for the longest time
Metric to measure effective memory access time considering cache hits and misses
A cache for virtual-to-physical address translations
Fast memory used for cache, maintains data while power is on without refresh
Main memory type, needs periodic refresh, slower than SRAM but cheaper

visualTips.visualization: Draw cache as a table with rows (lines/sets) and columns (tag, data, valid, dirty). Tag is like a name label, data is the actual content, valid is a 'checked' box, dirty is a 'modified' stamp.
For a 32-bit address with 64KB cache, 64-byte blocks: Tag | Set Index | Offset. Imagine a mailing address: Tag = House number, Set = Street, Offset = Room number within the house.
Draw inverted pyramid - Top (smallest, fastest): Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → SSD/HDD. Width increases going down, height (speed) decreases going down.
Think of cache as a library desk (cache) vs. main stacks (memory). If book at desk → quick (hit). If not → walk to stacks (miss penalty). You want most-used books at the desk.

analogies.analogy: L1 cache is like a counter where you keep frequently used ingredients while cooking. Main memory is the pantry. Instead of walking to pantry every time (slow), you keep items at counter (fast) for quick access. When counter is full, you replace least-used items.

Finding a book you need is like a cache hit if it's on the library desk (cache). If not there, you must go to the stacks (main memory) - that's a miss. The time taken to get the book is the miss penalty.

Direct mapping is like each floor having exactly one room for a specific guest type (always conflicts). Fully associative is like any room can have any guest (but requires checking all rooms). Set-associative is like each floor (set) has multiple rooms (ways) - guests of that type can go to any room on their floor.

Write-through is like taking notes in your personal notebook AND the class master copy simultaneously (safe but slower). Write-back is like just updating your personal notes and only updating the master copy when you leave (faster but risky if you lose your notes).
